author: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy
book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

title: Evil Alone is Corruption. Corruption is Not Nature, But Contrary to Nature. Corruption Implies Previous Good.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: For who can doubt that the whole of that which is called evil is nothing else than corruption?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Different evils may, indeed, be called by different names; but that which is the evil of all things in which any evil is perceptible is corruption.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: So the corruption of an educated mind is ignorance; the corruption of a prudent mind is imprudence; the corruption of a just mind, injustice; the corruption of a brave mind, cowardice; the corruption of a calm, peaceful mind, cupidity, fear, sorrow, pride.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Again, in a living body, the corruption of health is pain and disease; the corruption of strength is exhaustion; the corruption of rest is toil.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Again, in any corporeal thing, the corruption of beauty is ugliness; the corruption of straightness is crookedness; the corruption of order is confusion; the corruption of entireness is disseverance, or fracture, or diminution.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: It would be long and laborious to mention by name all the corruptions of the things here mentioned, and of countless other things; for in many cases the words may apply to the mind as well as to the body, and in innumerable cases the corruption has a distinct name of its own.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: But enough has been said to show that corruption does harm only as displacing the natural condition; and so, that corruption is not nature, but against nature.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: And if corruption is the only evil to be found anywhere, and if corruption is not nature, no nature is evil.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: But if, perchance, you cannot follow this, consider again, that whatever is corrupted is deprived of some good: for if it were not corrupted, it would be incorrupt; or if it could not in any way be corrupted, it would be incorruptible.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Now, if corruption is an evil, both incorruption and incorruptibility must be good things.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: We are not, however, speaking at present of incorruptible nature, but of things which admit of corruption, and which, while not corrupted, may be called incorrupt, but not incorruptible.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: That alone can be called incorruptible which not only is not corrupted, but also cannot in any part be corrupted.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Whatever things, then, being incorrupt, but liable to corruption, begin to be corrupted, are deprived of the good which they had as incorrupt.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Nor is this a slight good, for corruption is a great evil.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: And the continued increase of corruption implies the continued presence of good, of which they may be deprived.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Accordingly, the natures supposed to exist in the region of darkness must have been either corruptible or incorruptible.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If they were incorruptible, they were in possession of a good than which nothing is higher.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If they were corruptible, they were either corrupted or not corrupted.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If they were not corrupted, they were incorrupt, to say which of anything is to give it great praise.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If they were corrupted, they were deprived of this great good of incorruption; but the deprivation implies the previous possession of the good they are deprived of; and if they possessed this good, they were not the perfection of evil, and consequently all the Manichæan story is a falsehood.
